events_2027.name: Saphala Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Saphala Ekadashi falls during the Krishna Paksha of the Pausha month and is dedicated to Lord Vishnu. Devotees fast from sunrise to the next morning's sunrise, abstaining from grains and beans, and offer fruits to the deity. It is believed that observing this Ekadashi brings success and fulfillment in all endeavors. The night vigil with devotional songs and readings from the Vishnu Purana is considered especially meritorious.

events_2027.name: Guru Gobind Singh Ji Parkash Purab
events_2027.description: This day celebrates the birth anniversary of Guru Gobind Singh Ji, the tenth and last human Guru of the Sikhs, known for establishing the Khalsa in 1699. He was a warrior, poet, philosopher, and spiritual leader who gave the Sikhs their distinctive identity with the five articles of faith (Five Ks). Gurdwaras hold Akhand Path, Kirtan Darbars, and Nagar Kirtan processions, followed by community Langar. His teachings of courage, equality, and standing up against tyranny continue to define the Sikh way of life.

events_2027.name: Guru Gobind Singh Ji Birthday
events_2027.description: Many Hindus also celebrate the birthday of Guru Gobind Singh Ji, recognizing his immense contribution to protecting Hindu dharma from Mughal persecution. His father Guru Tegh Bahadur sacrificed his life to protect the sacred thread and tilak of Kashmiri Pandits. The tenth Guru's warrior poetry, including the Chandi di Var and Bachitar Natak, draws extensively from Hindu mythology. Interfaith celebrations on this day reflect the deep historical bonds between Hinduism and Sikhism.

events_2027.name: Pausha Amavasya
events_2027.description: Pausha Amavasya is the new moon day in the Hindu month of Pausha. Devotees perform Tarpan and Pind Daan rituals to honor departed ancestors and seek their blessings. Bathing in sacred rivers on this day is considered highly auspicious for spiritual purification. Many observe a fast and visit temples to pray for the well-being of their family and the peace of ancestral souls.

events_2027.name: Lohri
events_2027.description: Lohri is a vibrant Punjabi winter harvest festival celebrated with bonfires, music, and dance on the eve of Makara Sankranti. Families gather around the bonfire to offer peanuts, popcorn, rewri, and gajak to the fire god Agni, seeking blessings for prosperity. The festival holds special significance for newlywed couples and families with newborn children. Traditional Bhangra and Gidda dances enliven the celebration as the community marks the passing of the winter solstice.

events_2027.name: Makara Sankranti
events_2027.description: Makara Sankranti marks the sun's transition into the zodiac sign of Capricorn, signaling the end of the winter solstice and the beginning of longer days. It is celebrated across India with kite flying, bonfires, river bathing, and the exchange of sesame-jaggery sweets symbolizing warmth and togetherness. In Gujarat, the sky fills with colorful kites during the Uttarayan festival, while in the south it coincides with Pongal. This solar festival is dedicated to Surya, the Sun God, and heralds the auspicious Uttarayana period.

events_2027.name: Pongal
events_2027.description: Pongal is the four-day Tamil harvest festival giving thanks to nature, the Sun God, and cattle for a bountiful harvest. The signature dish of rice boiled with milk and jaggery is allowed to overflow the pot as a symbol of abundance and prosperity. On Mattu Pongal, cows and bulls are decorated with garlands and paint and worshipped for their service to agriculture. Families draw elaborate Kolam designs, wear new clothes, and gather for feasts to celebrate the harvest season.

events_2027.name: Maghi
events_2027.description: Maghi commemorates the sacrifice of the Forty Immortals (Chali Mukte), the forty Sikh warriors who fought valiantly in the Battle of Muktsar in 1705 under Guru Gobind Singh Ji. Sikhs visit Gurdwara Sri Muktsar Sahib to pay homage to these brave soldiers who had earlier deserted the Guru but returned to sacrifice their lives. The day begins with early morning prayers and Ardas, followed by Kirtan and Langar. It serves as a powerful reminder of courage, redemption, and unwavering faith.

events_2027.name: Meru Trayodashi
events_2027.description: Meru Trayodashi commemorates the day the first Tirthankara, Rishabhadeva, attained Nirvana and liberation from the cycle of birth and death. Jains visit temples to perform Abhishekam of the idols and offer prayers reflecting on the path of non-attachment. Special pujas dedicated to Mount Meru, regarded as the center of the universe in Jain cosmology, are conducted with great devotion. It is a day of deep contemplation on the ultimate spiritual goal of Moksha.

events_2027.name: Pausha Putrada Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Pausha Putrada Ekadashi, also known as Vaikuntha Ekadashi in some traditions, falls during the Shukla Paksha of Pausha month. It is especially observed by couples desiring a virtuous son, as the name suggests granting the boon of progeny. Devotees worship Lord Vishnu with tulsi leaves, flowers, and incense while maintaining a strict fast. Reading or listening to the Putrada Ekadashi Vrat Katha is considered essential for receiving the full spiritual benefits.

events_2027.name: Pausha Purnima
events_2027.description: Pausha Purnima marks the full moon of the Pausha month and the beginning of the austerity period leading to the Magha Mela. Thousands of devotees bathe in holy rivers like the Ganges at Prayagraj to purify their souls and accumulate spiritual merit. This day also marks the start of Kalpavas for ascetics, a period of simple living and meditation by the riverbanks. Charity and donations made on Pausha Purnima are believed to yield immense spiritual rewards.

events_2027.name: Mauni Amavasya
events_2027.description: Although astronomically the Amavasya falls on February 6 this year, the preparatory observance of silence begins in the Magha month. Devotees practice Mauna Vrat, a vow of total silence, to discipline the mind and connect with the inner self. It is considered the most auspicious day for bathing at the Sangam in Prayagraj. Millions of pilgrims gather for the sacred dip, believing it purifies the soul of accumulated sins across lifetimes.

events_2027.name: Shattila Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Shattila Ekadashi emphasizes the spiritual significance of sesame seeds (Til) used in six sacred ways: bathing, anointing, offering in fire, donating, eating, and gifting. Dedicated to Lord Vishnu, this fast cleanses the body and soul of accumulated impurities and sins. Devotees prepare sesame-based foods and distribute them as charity to the needy. Strict fasting from grains is maintained to ensure maximum spiritual merit and divine blessings.

events_2027.name: Magha Amavasya
events_2027.description: Magha Amavasya is the new moon day in the sacred month of Magha, highly revered for performing Shraddha and Tarpan rituals for departed ancestors. Devotees bathe in sacred rivers and offer prayers seeking liberation and peace for ancestral souls. This day also falls as Shani Amavasya, making it especially significant for appeasing the planet Saturn through charitable acts. Fasting and acts of generosity on this day are believed to remove obstacles and bring karmic balance.

events_2027.name: Mauni Amavasya
events_2027.description: Jain communities observe Mauni Amavasya as a day of silence and inner reflection, aligning with the Hindu observance but emphasizing the Jain practice of speech control. Monks and lay practitioners maintain complete silence throughout the day, focusing on meditation and self-study. The Jain emphasis on Maun (silence) as a form of Tapas (austerity) makes this day particularly significant for spiritual advancement. Devotees visit temples for darshan and engage in charitable activities as expressions of compassion.

events_2027.name: Vasant Panchami
events_2027.description: Vasant Panchami celebrates the arrival of spring and is dedicated to Goddess Saraswati, the deity of knowledge, music, and arts. Devotees dress in yellow, symbolizing the vibrancy of mustard fields in bloom, and place books and instruments near Saraswati's altar for blessings. Schools and educational institutions hold special prayers, and young children are initiated into learning on this auspicious day. It marks the beginning of preparations for the Holi festival, forty days away.

events_2027.name: Magha Puja
events_2027.description: Magha Puja, also known as Sangha Day, commemorates the spontaneous gathering of 1,250 enlightened Arahants who came to hear the Buddha preach the Ovadapatimokkha, the core principles of Buddhism. Celebrated with candlelit processions around temples called Wien Tien, this day emphasizes the importance of the monastic Sangha. Buddhists commit to doing good, purifying the mind, and abstaining from harmful actions. It is one of the most important Theravada Buddhist holidays observed across Southeast Asia.

events_2027.name: Maha Shivaratri
events_2027.description: Maha Shivaratri, the Great Night of Lord Shiva, is observed with night-long vigils, fasting, and the chanting of Om Namah Shivaya in Shiva temples worldwide. Devotees pour milk, honey, water, and bel leaves over the Shiva Lingam throughout the night in elaborate Abhishekam ceremonies. The festival celebrates the cosmic dance of creation and destruction and is believed to be the night when Shiva performed the Tandava. It is considered the most auspicious time for spiritual seekers to transcend darkness and ignorance.

events_2027.name: Parinirvana Day
events_2027.description: Parinirvana Day, also called Nirvana Day, marks the passing of the Buddha and his entry into Final Nirvana, liberation from the cycle of rebirth. It is a time for deep reflection on the impermanence of all conditioned things and the inevitability of death. Buddhists read the Mahaparinirvana Sutra and gather for meditation retreats focusing on the Buddha's final teachings. His last words, urging diligence in spiritual practice, are contemplated by practitioners worldwide.

events_2027.name: Jaya Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Jaya Ekadashi, observed during the Shukla Paksha of the Magha month, is renowned for its power to absolve even the gravest of sins. According to the Padma Purana, this Ekadashi liberated a celestial being cursed by Indra, restoring them to heaven. Devotees maintain a complete fast, worship Lord Vishnu, and stay awake through the night with devotional songs. Observing this Ekadashi is believed to grant victory over the cycle of birth and death.

events_2027.name: Chinese New Year (Buddhist)
events_2027.description: While primarily a cultural festival, Chinese New Year is celebrated by Mahayana Buddhists as a time of spiritual renewal and gratitude. Devotees visit temples to light incense for ancestors, offer prayers for prosperity, and make merit through charitable donations. Homes are cleaned to sweep away bad luck, and vegetarian feasts are prepared as an offering of compassion. The festival emphasizes family reunion, honoring elders, and beginning the year with positive intentions and good karma.

events_2027.name: Losar (Tibetan New Year)
events_2027.description: Losar, the Tibetan New Year, is the most important festival in the Tibetan Buddhist calendar, celebrated over fifteen days. Monasteries and homes are cleaned, special ritual cakes (Guthuk) are prepared, and prayer flags are hung to welcome the new year. The celebrations include Cham dances performed by monks in elaborate costumes and masks representing dharma protectors. Devotees visit temples to offer prayers, light butter lamps, and set intentions for spiritual growth in the coming year.

events_2027.name: Magha Purnima
events_2027.description: Magha Purnima is the full moon of the sacred Magha month, considered one of the holiest days for ritual bathing in the Ganges and other sacred rivers. Devotees believe that charity, fasting, and worship performed on this day yield manifold spiritual rewards. In many regions, grand fairs and religious gatherings are organized near riverbanks and holy sites. This Purnima also marks the conclusion of the month-long Magha Snaan observed by devout Hindus.

events_2027.name: Magha Puja (Fourfold Assembly)
events_2027.description: Magha Puja celebrates the miraculous gathering where 1,250 Arahants came to the Buddha unbidden. The Buddha delivered the Ovadapatimokkha, a summary of Buddhist principles: cease from evil, do good, and purify the mind. Temples throughout Southeast Asia hold Wien Tien candlelit circumambulations of the main shrine hall. Devotees offer candles, flowers, and incense, recommitting themselves to the precepts and the Buddhist path.

events_2027.name: Vijaya Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Vijaya Ekadashi falls during the Krishna Paksha of the Phalguna month and is believed to bring victory in all undertakings. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, Lord Rama observed this Ekadashi before crossing the ocean to Lanka, ensuring his triumph over Ravana. Devotees fast strictly and offer prayers to Lord Vishnu with devotion, seeking success in their endeavors. It is especially recommended for those facing difficult challenges or embarking on important journeys.

events_2027.name: Phalguna Amavasya
events_2027.description: Phalguna Amavasya is the new moon day in the month of Phalguna, dedicated to performing Shraddha rituals and Tarpan for departed ancestors. As a Somvati Amavasya falling on Monday in 2027, it carries additional spiritual significance for worshipping Lord Shiva. Devotees fast, visit temples, and offer prayers for the peace of ancestral souls and family prosperity. Charity given on this day, especially to Brahmins and the needy, is considered highly meritorious.

events_2027.name: Phalguna Amavasya (Jain)
events_2027.description: Jain communities observe the Phalguna Amavasya with fasting, temple visits, and charitable activities emphasizing the Jain virtues of non-violence and compassion. Monks deliver special discourses on the significance of Amavasya in the Jain spiritual calendar. Devotees practice Santhara-like partial fasts and dedicate the day to inner purification. The darkness of the new moon serves as a metaphor for the ignorance that spiritual practice aims to overcome.

events_2027.name: Holi
events_2027.description: Holi, the Festival of Colors, celebrates the victory of good over evil, commemorating the burning of the demoness Holika and the devotion of Prahlada to Lord Vishnu. On the eve, Holika Dahan bonfires are lit to symbolize the destruction of evil forces, and the next day people joyfully throw colored powders and water at each other. The festival also celebrates the divine love of Radha and Krishna, with Mathura and Vrindavan hosting legendary celebrations. Holi breaks down social barriers as people of all backgrounds come together in a spirit of joy and forgiveness.

events_2027.name: Hola Mohalla
events_2027.description: Hola Mohalla is a three-day Sikh festival established by Guru Gobind Singh Ji in 1701 as a gathering for martial arts displays and military exercises. Held at Anandpur Sahib the day after Holi, Nihang Singhs demonstrate sword fighting, horse riding, archery, and other martial skills. The event includes Kirtan, devotional music, and massive Langar serving thousands of visitors. It embodies the Sikh spirit of combining spiritual devotion with the warrior tradition of Saint-Soldiers.

events_2027.name: Nanakshahi New Year
events_2027.description: The Nanakshahi New Year marks the beginning of the Sikh calendar year on Chet 1 (March 14), aligning with the start of the Sikh calendar established to give Sikhs an independent system for tracking religious observances. Gurdwaras hold special prayers, Kirtan, and Langar to mark the occasion. The Nanakshahi calendar was formally adopted by the SGPC in 2003, calculated from the birth year of Guru Nanak Dev Ji. It is a day of renewal and recommitment to the teachings of the Sikh Gurus.

events_2027.name: Amalaki Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Amalaki Ekadashi, celebrated during the Shukla Paksha of Phalguna, is named after the sacred Amla (Indian Gooseberry) tree, which is worshipped as an embodiment of Lord Vishnu. Devotees fast, perform puja around the Amla tree, and offer its fruits to the deity. According to the Brahmanda Purana, observing this Ekadashi absolves sins and grants liberation. The Amla fruit itself is revered in Ayurveda as a source of rejuvenation and vitality.

events_2027.name: Guru Hargobind Sahib Ji Joti Jot
events_2027.description: Sikhs observe the Joti Jot (merging with the divine light) of Guru Hargobind Sahib Ji, the sixth Guru who introduced the concept of the warrior-saint into Sikhism. He carried two swords of Miri and Piri, symbolizing temporal and spiritual authority, and fought several battles to defend the faith. Gurdwaras hold solemn prayers and Kirtan to honor his legacy of bravery and devotion. His life exemplifies the Sikh principle that spiritual leadership must be paired with the courage to protect the vulnerable.

events_2027.name: Phalguna Purnima
events_2027.description: Phalguna Purnima is the full moon of the Phalguna month, closely associated with the Holi celebrations in northern India. In the Vaishnava tradition, this day is also observed as Gaura Purnima, marking the appearance of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Devotees perform Satyanarayan Puja, observe fasts, and visit temples to seek blessings. Bathing in sacred rivers and giving charity on this Purnima is believed to grant spiritual merit and divine grace.

events_2027.name: Chaitra Shukla Pratipada (Hindu New Year)
events_2027.description: Chaitra Shukla Pratipada marks the beginning of the Hindu New Year according to the Vikram Samvat calendar. It is celebrated as Gudi Padwa in Maharashtra, Ugadi in Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh, and Navreh in Kashmir. Families erect the Gudi, a silk cloth adorned with neem leaves, sugar, and a copper pot, symbolizing victory and prosperity. Special dishes combining sweet, sour, and bitter flavors represent the varied experiences that the new year will bring.

events_2027.name: Ugadi
events_2027.description: Ugadi marks the New Year for the people of Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Telangana, celebrated on Chaitra Shukla Pratipada. The traditional Ugadi Pachadi, made with six tastes representing the different facets of life, is prepared and consumed as a reminder to accept all experiences equally. Homes are cleaned and decorated with mango leaf torans, and new Panchangam readings predict the year ahead. The day begins with oil baths, new clothes, and temple visits to seek blessings for the coming year.

events_2027.name: Navapad Oli Begins (Jain)
events_2027.description: The Ashwin Navapad Oli is a nine-day period of austere devotion observed by Jains twice a year. Devotees worship the Navapad, the nine supreme entities of Jain philosophy, which include Arihant, Siddha, Acharya, Upadhyay, and Sadhu. Strict dietary discipline is maintained, with devotees eating only once a day and avoiding green vegetables. The Oli represents the Jain commitment to regular periods of heightened spiritual practice and self-purification.

events_2027.name: Papamochani Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Papamochani Ekadashi, falling during the Krishna Paksha of Chaitra, is named for its power to destroy all sins (Papa). According to the Bhavisyottara Purana, observing this fast redeems even those who have committed grave transgressions. Devotees worship Lord Vishnu with deep devotion, maintain a strict fast, and keep vigil through the night. This Ekadashi is the last before the Hindu New Year, offering a chance for spiritual cleansing before the fresh start.

events_2027.name: Chaitra Amavasya
events_2027.description: Chaitra Amavasya is the new moon day in the first month of the Hindu calendar, considered sacred for performing ancestral rites and Tarpan. Devotees visit pilgrimage sites and sacred rivers to offer prayers for the departed souls of their family. This Amavasya falls close to the beginning of Chaitra Navratri, making it a time of spiritual preparation and purification. Charity, fasting, and meditation performed on this day are believed to bring peace and spiritual advancement.

events_2027.name: Rama Navami
events_2027.description: Rama Navami celebrates the birth of Lord Rama, the seventh avatar of Vishnu and the hero of the epic Ramayana. Devotees fast, recite the Ramayana, and perform special pujas at temples, placing a cradle for baby Rama at noon, the believed hour of his birth. The festival falls on the ninth day of Chaitra Navratri, culminating the nine-day worship of divine energy. Processions with decorated chariots carrying idols of Rama, Sita, Lakshmana, and Hanuman fill the streets with devotional fervor.

events_2027.name: Chaitra Navratri Begins
events_2027.description: Chaitra Navratri is the nine-night spring festival dedicated to the nine forms of Goddess Durga, beginning on Chaitra Shukla Pratipada. Each day honors a different aspect of the Divine Mother with specific rituals, colors, and offerings. Devotees observe strict fasts, perform Durga Saptashati recitations, and conduct Kanya Pujan on the ninth day. This Navratri is especially significant for spiritual seekers as the spring energy supports new beginnings and inner transformation.

events_2027.name: Gudi Padwa
events_2027.description: Gudi Padwa is the Maharashtrian New Year celebrated on Chaitra Shukla Pratipada, marking the beginning of a new Hindu calendar year. Families erect the Gudi, a bright green or yellow silk cloth tied to a bamboo stick adorned with neem leaves, mango leaves, a garland, and topped with an inverted copper pot. The Gudi symbolizes victory, prosperity, and the raising of Brahma's flag of creation. Puran Poli and Shrikhand are prepared as festive delicacies, and homes are decorated with rangoli designs.

events_2027.name: Navapada Oli Begins
events_2027.description: Chaitra Navapada Oli is a nine-day Jain festival of austerity and devotion observed during the Shukla Paksha of the Chaitra month. During this period, Jains follow strict dietary restrictions, eating only once a day and abstaining from green vegetables. Devotees worship the Navapada, the nine supreme entities of Jain philosophy, through meditation and prayer. The Oli period is considered highly auspicious for spiritual advancement and self-purification.

events_2027.name: Songkran (Theravada New Year)
events_2027.description: Songkran, the Theravada Buddhist New Year, is celebrated in Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, and Myanmar as a time of purification and renewal. Devotees pour scented water over Buddha statues in a ritual of cleansing and visit temples to offer food to monks and make merit. The tradition of pouring water on elders' hands to receive their blessings is a cornerstone of the celebration. Communities release captive birds and fish as acts of compassion, symbolizing freedom and the release of bad karma.

events_2027.name: Hanuman Jayanti
events_2027.description: Hanuman Jayanti celebrates the birth of Lord Hanuman, the devoted servant of Lord Rama known for his immeasurable strength, wisdom, and devotion. Temples are decorated, and the Hanuman Chalisa and Sundara Kanda from the Ramayana are recited throughout the day. Devotees apply sindoor (vermillion) to Hanuman idols and distribute prasad of laddoos. The festival inspires qualities of selfless service, courage, and unwavering devotion to the divine.

events_2027.name: Baisakhi
events_2027.description: Baisakhi, also known as Vaisakhi, commemorates the founding of the Khalsa by Guru Gobind Singh Ji in 1699 and marks the Punjabi New Year. Gurdwaras hold special prayers and Amrit Sanchar (initiation) ceremonies, while the Nishan Sahib (Sikh flag) is ceremonially replaced. The festival coincides with the harvest season in Punjab, celebrated with Bhangra, music, and communal Langar. It is one of the most important dates in the Sikh calendar, symbolizing courage, sacrifice, and community.

events_2027.name: Ambedkar Jayanti
events_2027.description: Ambedkar Jayanti celebrates the birth of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, the architect of the Indian Constitution who embraced Buddhism along with millions of followers in 1956. His Navayana Buddhism movement brought dignity and equality to millions of marginalized people through the Buddhist path. Followers visit stupas and viharas, pay tribute to his statue, and participate in discussions on social justice and Buddhist values. The day honors his vision of a casteless society grounded in the Buddhist principles of compassion and equality.

events_2027.name: Kamada Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Kamada Ekadashi falls during the Shukla Paksha of Chaitra and is believed to fulfill all desires (Kama) of the devotee. According to the Varaha Purana, this Ekadashi freed a celestial couple from a curse, restoring their divine forms. Devotees worship Lord Vishnu with flowers, tulsi leaves, and incense while observing a complete fast. It is the first Ekadashi of the Hindu New Year, making it especially potent for setting spiritual intentions.

events_2027.name: Guru Angad Dev Ji Joti Jot / Guru Amar Das Ji Gurgaddi
events_2027.description: This date in the Nanakshahi calendar commemorates the passing of Guru Angad Dev Ji and the simultaneous ascension of Guru Amar Das Ji to the Guruship. It also marks the passing of Guru Harkrishan Sahib Ji and the ascension of Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji. Gurdwaras hold special joint prayers honoring the seamless spiritual succession of the Sikh Gurus. The day reminds Sikhs that the divine light of Nanak passed unchanged through each successive Guru.

events_2027.name: Mahavir Jayanti
events_2027.description: Mahavir Jayanti celebrates the birth of Lord Mahavira, the 24th and last Tirthankara who established the foundational principles of Jain Dharma. Grand processions carry ornate idols of Mahavira through the streets accompanied by music and devotional chanting. Temples perform Abhishekam with milk, saffron water, and sandalwood paste, and devotees practice charity and compassion. The day serves as a reminder of Mahavira's teachings of Ahimsa (non-violence), Satya (truth), and Aparigraha (non-attachment).

events_2027.name: Phalguna Purnima (Chaitra)
events_2027.description: This full moon day falls in the transition month and is observed by many as the culmination of Chaitra Navratri period. Devotees perform Satyanarayan Puja and offer prayers at temples seeking divine grace for their families. Bathing in sacred rivers and charitable acts are considered highly meritorious on this day. In many regions, fairs and community gatherings are organized to celebrate the spiritual significance of the Purnima.

events_2027.name: Akshaya Tritiya
events_2027.description: Akshaya Tritiya is considered one of the most auspicious days in the Hindu calendar, when any act of merit is believed to bring infinite and imperishable rewards. It commemorates the day when Lord Parashurama, the sixth avatar of Vishnu, appeared on earth. Devotees begin new ventures, purchase gold, and perform charity, as the word Akshaya means that which never diminishes. Vedic rituals, Pitru Tarpan, and worship of Goddess Lakshmi are integral parts of the celebration.

events_2027.name: Akshaya Tritiya
events_2027.description: For Jains, Akshaya Tritiya holds special significance as the day when the first Tirthankara, Rishabhadeva, broke his year-long fast with sugarcane juice offered by King Shreyans. This event established the tradition of giving alms to monks and ascetics, making it the holiest day for charitable giving. Jain temples organize special Abhishekam ceremonies and devotees observe fasting and meditation. The day symbolizes the imperishable nature of the soul and the spiritual merit of generosity.

events_2027.name: Varuthini Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Varuthini Ekadashi, observed during the Krishna Paksha of Vaishakha, is named for its protective power, shielding devotees from sin and misfortune. According to the Bhavishya Purana, this Ekadashi grants more merit than visiting all holy pilgrimage sites combined. Devotees worship Lord Vamana, the dwarf avatar of Vishnu, with special prayers and offerings. Strict fasting and night vigils are maintained to receive the full spiritual protection promised by this sacred observance.

events_2027.name: Guru Arjan Dev Ji Parkash Purab
events_2027.description: This day celebrates the birth anniversary of Guru Arjan Dev Ji, the fifth Sikh Guru who compiled the Adi Granth (Guru Granth Sahib) and built the Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple). He was the first Sikh martyr who sacrificed his life rather than compromise his faith. Gurdwaras hold Akhand Path and special Kirtan sessions to honor his immense contributions. His teachings of patience, acceptance of divine will, and selfless service continue to inspire millions.

events_2027.name: Vaishakha Amavasya
events_2027.description: Vaishakha Amavasya is the new moon day of the sacred Vaishakha month, highly auspicious for performing ancestral rites and holy bathing. Devotees visit Varanasi, Haridwar, and other pilgrimage sites to offer Tarpan for departed souls. According to the scriptures, charity and religious observances on this day yield manifold spiritual rewards. Many also observe it as an opportunity for introspection and renewing spiritual commitments.

events_2027.name: Vaishakha Amavasya
events_2027.description: The Vaishakha Amavasya new moon falls in one of the holiest months of the Hindu calendar, making it an especially potent day for ancestral worship. Devotees perform elaborate Tarpan ceremonies at sacred rivers and pilgrimage sites, offering water, sesame, and rice to departed ancestors. This Amavasya is considered second only to the Mahalaya Amavasya in its significance for Pitru Tarpan. Fasting and charitable acts performed on this day are said to bring blessings from both the divine and ancestral realms.

events_2027.name: Varshi Tap Parna
events_2027.description: Varshi Tap Parna marks the breaking of the year-long alternate-day fasting undertaken by devout Jain practitioners. Those who complete this extraordinary feat of austerity break their fast on this day with sugarcane juice, mirroring the first alms received by Rishabhadeva. The occasion is celebrated with great reverence as communities honor the tapasvis (those who completed the fast) with processions and ceremonies. It exemplifies the Jain emphasis on Tapas (austerity) as a means of burning away karma and advancing toward liberation.

events_2027.name: Buddha Purnima (Vesak)
events_2027.description: Buddha Purnima, also known as Vesak, is the most sacred Buddhist festival commemorating the birth, enlightenment, and passing of Gautama Buddha. Monasteries and temples worldwide are decorated with colorful flags and flowers, and devotees gather for meditation, chanting, and Dharma talks. In Bodh Gaya, thousands of pilgrims circumambulate the Mahabodhi Temple under the descendant of the original Bodhi Tree. Acts of generosity, releasing caged animals, and offering food to monks are integral to the celebrations.

events_2027.name: Buddha Purnima
events_2027.description: Hindus revere Buddha Purnima as the birthday of Gautama Buddha, considered the ninth avatar of Lord Vishnu in many Hindu traditions. Devotees visit Buddhist temples and sacred sites, offering flowers, incense, and fruit to Buddha statues. The Vaishakha Purnima is considered one of the holiest full moon days, and sacred river bathing is undertaken with great devotion. It is a day for practicing compassion, mindfulness, and non-violence across all traditions.

events_2027.name: Mohini Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Mohini Ekadashi, falling during the Shukla Paksha of Vaishakha, is named after the enchanting female form assumed by Lord Vishnu. According to the Surya Purana, observing this Ekadashi destroys the most terrible sins and grants liberation. Devotees fast with great devotion and worship Lord Vishnu in his Mohini avatar form. It is particularly recommended for those seeking to overcome past karmic debts and spiritual obstacles.

events_2027.name: Mahavira Kevala Jnana Divas
events_2027.description: This day commemorates Lord Mahavira's attainment of Kevala Jnana (omniscience), the infinite and absolute knowledge that marks the highest spiritual achievement in Jain tradition. According to Jain texts, Mahavira achieved this supreme knowledge after twelve years of rigorous penance and meditation. Temples hold special pujas, and scholars deliver discourses on the significance of Kevala Jnana and the path to achieving it. Devotees fast and meditate, aspiring to follow in the Tirthankara's footsteps toward spiritual perfection.

events_2027.name: Guru Amar Das Ji Parkash Purab
events_2027.description: This day marks the birth anniversary of Guru Amar Das Ji, the third Sikh Guru who established the Langar tradition of communal dining regardless of caste or creed. He worked to abolish the practice of Sati and promoted the equality of women in Sikh society. Gurdwaras hold special prayers, Kirtan, and community Langar to honor his legacy. His message of social reform and equality remains a cornerstone of Sikh values.

events_2027.name: Vaishakha Purnima
events_2027.description: Vaishakha Purnima is observed by many Buddhist traditions as the full moon day commemorating key events in the life of the Buddha. Some traditions celebrate the birth, enlightenment, and Parinirvana on this single day, while others distribute these across different dates. Temples are illuminated with oil lamps and lanterns, and devotees participate in silent meditation sessions. The day emphasizes the core Buddhist practices of generosity, morality, and mental cultivation.

events_2027.name: Shravana Purnima Preparation
events_2027.description: Jain devotees begin preparations for upcoming festivals with special prayers and temple visits during this period. Communities organize Swadhyay sessions focusing on the teachings of the Tirthankaras and the path to liberation. Monks and nuns deliver discourses on the principles of Ahimsa, Aparigraha, and Anekantavada. It is a time for deepening one's understanding of Jain philosophy and renewing spiritual commitments.

events_2027.name: Apara Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Apara Ekadashi, observed during the Krishna Paksha of Jyeshtha, is renowned for granting limitless (Apara) merit to the observer. According to the Brahmanda Purana, this Ekadashi is equivalent to performing multiple Ashwamedha Yagnas and visiting sacred pilgrimage sites. Devotees worship Lord Trivikrama, a form of Vishnu, and maintain a strict fast throughout the day. It is especially recommended for those seeking to overcome guilt and find spiritual redemption.

events_2027.name: Jyeshtha Amavasya
events_2027.description: Jyeshtha Amavasya is the new moon day of the Jyeshtha month, considered powerful for performing Tarpan rituals and ancestral worship. Devotees visit sacred rivers and offer prayers for the liberation of departed souls from the cycle of rebirth. The day is also associated with Shani worship, as charitable acts on Amavasya help mitigate the adverse effects of Saturn. Fasting and meditation are observed to strengthen spiritual discipline and connect with the divine.

events_2027.name: Ganga Dussehra
events_2027.description: Ganga Dussehra celebrates the descent of the holy river Ganges from heaven to earth, brought down by the penance of King Bhagiratha to liberate his ancestors. Devotثes gather at ghats along the Ganges, especially in Haridwar, Varanasi, and Prayagraj, to take ritual baths and float lamps on the river. It is believed that bathing in the Ganges on this day washes away ten types of sins. Special Aarti ceremonies are performed at major temples along the riverbanks.

events_2027.name: Poson Poya
events_2027.description: Poson Poya is a major Buddhist festival in Sri Lanka commemorating the arrival of Buddhism to the island through the mission of Arahat Mahinda, son of Emperor Ashoka. Celebrated on the June full moon day, thousands of pilgrims climb Mihintale, the hill where Mahinda first met King Devanampiyatissa and converted him. Temples and streets are decorated with colorful lanterns and illuminations, and Dansalas (free food stalls) line the roads. It is second only to Vesak in importance in the Sri Lankan Buddhist calendar.

events_2027.name: Nirjala Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Nirjala Ekadashi, falling during the Shukla Paksha of Jyeshtha, is the most austere of all Ekadashis as devotees fast without even water for the entire day. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, Bhima was granted the merit of all 24 Ekadashis by observing just this one waterless fast. Despite falling in the peak of summer heat, devoted observers maintain their fast from sunrise to the next sunrise. The spiritual reward is considered immeasurable, equivalent to observing all Ekadashis in the year.

events_2027.name: Guru Arjan Dev Ji Shaheedi Divas
events_2027.description: This solemn day marks the martyrdom of Guru Arjan Dev Ji, the fifth Sikh Guru, who was tortured and executed by Mughal Emperor Jahangir in 1606 for refusing to alter the Adi Granth. He accepted his suffering with grace, teaching that one should accept the will of the Almighty without resistance. Gurdwaras hold special prayers and serve cold, sweet beverages (Chabeel) to passers-by to commemorate his sacrifice. His martyrdom inspired the transformation of Sikhism from a purely spiritual movement to one of warrior-saints.

events_2027.name: Jyeshtha Purnima (Vat Purnima)
events_2027.description: Jyeshtha Purnima, also known as Vat Purnima, is the full moon of the Jyeshtha month when married women worship the Banyan tree (Vat Vriksha) for the long life and well-being of their husbands. The legend of Savitri, who won back her husband's life from Yama, the god of death, is central to the observance. Women tie sacred threads around the Banyan tree and circumambulate it while reciting prayers. It is also observed as Deva Snana Purnima in Odisha with the ritual bathing of deities.

events_2027.name: Snatra Puja
events_2027.description: Snatra Puja is a ceremonial bathing ritual performed for Jain Tirthankara idols, reenacting the Abhishekam performed by the heavenly beings at the birth of each Tirthankara. Devotees bathe the idol with water, milk, sandalwood paste, and saffron while chanting sacred verses from the Snatra Puja text. The ritual symbolizes the purification of the soul and the reverence for the supreme beings who have conquered worldly attachments. It is performed with great devotion on special occasions and full moon days throughout the year.

events_2027.name: Guru Hargobind Sahib Ji Parkash Purab
events_2027.description: This day celebrates the birth anniversary of Guru Hargobind Sahib Ji, the sixth Sikh Guru who introduced the concept of Miri-Piri, the balance of spiritual and temporal authority. He wore two swords representing spiritual sovereignty (Piri) and worldly power (Miri), transforming the Sikh community into a martial tradition. Gurdwaras hold Kirtan and Langar to honor his legacy of courage and faith. His construction of the Akal Takht opposite the Harmandir Sahib symbolized this dual authority.

events_2027.name: Devshayani Ekadashi Preparation
events_2027.description: As the Chaturmas period approaches, devotees prepare for the most important shift in the Hindu ritual calendar. Families plan auspicious events like weddings and sacred thread ceremonies before Lord Vishnu enters his cosmic sleep. Temples organize special Vishnu Pujas and Bhagavata Saptaha readings in the lead-up to Devshayani Ekadashi. This preparation period emphasizes the importance of completing worldly celebrations before the four months of spiritual intensification begin.

events_2027.name: Yogini Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Yogini Ekadashi, observed during the Krishna Paksha of Ashadha, is dedicated to Lord Vishnu and is known for its power to cure physical ailments and spiritual afflictions. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, this Ekadashi cured a devotee of a terrible disease caused by past-life sins. Devotees worship Lord Vishnu with flowers, fruits, and incense while maintaining a strict fast. Observing this Ekadashi is said to grant health, longevity, and freedom from suffering.

events_2027.name: Ashadha Amavasya
events_2027.description: Ashadha Amavasya marks the new moon of the Ashadha month, a significant day for performing ancestral rites and Pitru Tarpan. Devotees visit holy rivers and sacred sites to offer prayers and seek blessings from their departed forebears. This Amavasya also marks the beginning of the Chaturmas period for many Hindu ascetics and Jain monks. Fasting and meditation on this day are believed to strengthen one's connection to the spiritual realm.

events_2027.name: Rath Yatra
events_2027.description: Rath Yatra is the grand chariot festival of Lord Jagannath, celebrated most magnificently at Puri in Odisha, where three massive chariots carry the deities through the streets. Millions of devotees pull the ropes of the chariots as an act of devotion, with the procession stretching for kilometers. The three deities, Jagannath, Balabhadra, and Subhadra, journey from the main temple to the Gundicha Temple. This ancient festival symbolizes the Lord's compassion in coming out among the people, regardless of caste or status.

events_2027.name: Chaturmas Begins (Jain)
events_2027.description: Chaturmas marks the beginning of the four-month monsoon retreat for Jain monks and nuns, who cease their wandering and settle in communities to deliver discourses. During this period, the lay community has increased access to monastic teachers and intensifies their own spiritual practice. Devotees attend daily Pravachans (spiritual discourses), practice additional fasts, and deepen their study of Jain scriptures. The Chaturmas period represents a time of heightened spiritual activity and community bonding in Jain society.

events_2027.name: Guru Purnima
events_2027.description: Guru Purnima honors the Guru, the spiritual teacher who illuminates the path from darkness to knowledge. Traditionally dedicated to the sage Vyasa who compiled the Vedas and authored the Mahabharata, this day is marked by expressions of gratitude toward all teachers. Disciples visit their Gurus bearing offerings of flowers and fruits, and spiritual organizations hold special discourses and meditation sessions. In the yogic tradition, it marks the day Shiva became the first Guru, Adi Guru, transmitting yoga to the Saptarishis.

events_2027.name: Guru Purnima
events_2027.description: For Buddhists, Guru Purnima commemorates the day when the Buddha gave his first sermon at Sarnath, known as the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, setting the Wheel of Dharma in motion. This day honors the Buddha as the supreme teacher who showed the path to enlightenment and freedom from suffering. Buddhist monasteries hold special meditation sessions, Dharma talks, and Dana ceremonies. Practitioners renew their commitment to the Triple Gem of Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.

events_2027.name: Dharma Chakra Day
events_2027.description: Dharma Chakra Day, also known as Asadha Poornima, celebrates the Buddha's first discourse and the beginning of the Buddhist Sangha. The Indian government has designated this day for honoring the contributions of Buddhism to world civilization. Buddhist centers and monasteries organize meditation camps, Dharma discussions, and community outreach programs. Devotees meditate on the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path as the foundation of all Buddhist practice.

events_2027.name: Devshayani Ekadashi (Shayani Ekadashi)
events_2027.description: Devshayani Ekadashi marks the beginning of the Chaturmas period when Lord Vishnu is believed to enter his cosmic sleep on the serpent Shesha. From this day until Prabodhini Ekadashi in Kartik, auspicious ceremonies like marriages and housewarming are traditionally avoided. Devotees observe a strict fast and worship Lord Vishnu, seeking his blessings before his period of rest. This Ekadashi is also known as Hari Shayani Ekadashi and holds special significance in Pandharpur, Maharashtra.

events_2027.name: Ashadha Purnima
events_2027.description: Ashadha Purnima, marking the full moon of the Hindu month of Ashadha, is observed by Indian Buddhists as the day the Buddha first turned the Wheel of Dharma at Sarnath. The Indian government recognizes this as an important Buddhist observance honoring the establishment of the Four Noble Truths. Pilgrimages to Sarnath's Dhamek Stupa and Mulagandha Kuti Vihar attract Buddhist practitioners from around the world. Meditation retreats and Dharma study programs are organized to deepen practitioners' understanding of the core teachings.

events_2027.name: Ashadha Purnima
events_2027.description: Ashadha Purnima, also known as Vyasa Purnima, coincides with Guru Purnima and is considered the full moon of the rainy season month. It marks the beginning of the traditional retreat period for monks and ascetics across Hindu and Buddhist traditions. Devotees perform puja, offer charity, and engage in spiritual study throughout the day. In many temple traditions, this Purnima signals the start of intensive scripture reading and meditation practices.

events_2027.name: Ashadha Purnima (Esala Poya)
events_2027.description: Esala Poya is celebrated by Sri Lankan Buddhists on the Ashadha full moon, commemorating the Buddha's first sermon and the conception of Prince Siddhartha. The famous Esala Perahera festival in Kandy features a grand procession of decorated elephants, Kandyan dancers, fire jugglers, and the sacred Tooth Relic. Buddhist monks begin the Vassa retreat on this day, committing to three months of intensive practice. It is one of the most visually spectacular Buddhist celebrations in the world.

events_2027.name: Asalha Puja (Dharma Day)
events_2027.description: Asalha Puja, also known as Dharma Day, commemorates the Buddha's first sermon, the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, delivered to five ascetics at the Deer Park in Sarnath. This discourse set the Wheel of Dharma in motion and established the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path as the core of Buddhist teaching. The day also marks the founding of the Buddhist Sangha when Kondanna became the first disciple to attain enlightenment. Temples hold candlelit processions and all-day meditation sessions.

events_2027.name: Vassa (Buddhist Lent) Begins
events_2027.description: Vassa, the Buddhist Rains Retreat, begins the day after Asalha Puja and lasts for three months during the monsoon season. Monks traditionally remain in their monasteries during this period, dedicating themselves to intensive meditation and study. Lay Buddhists take this as an opportunity to deepen their own practice, often taking additional precepts and attending more frequent Dharma talks. The retreat echoes the Buddha's own practice of settling in one place during the rains to avoid harming insects and new plant growth.

events_2027.name: Kamika Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Kamika Ekadashi is observed during the Krishna Paksha of Shravana and is dedicated to the worship of Lord Vishnu with Tulsi leaves. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, this Ekadashi grants the same merit as donating a cow adorned with gold. Devotees fast strictly, perform elaborate pujas with Tulsi, and recite Vishnu Sahasranama throughout the day. It is especially significant during the Chaturmas period for those seeking spiritual purification.

events_2027.name: Shravana Amavasya (Hariyali Amavasya)
events_2027.description: Hariyali Amavasya, the new moon of the Shravana month, celebrates the lush greenery of the monsoon season and is dedicated to honoring ancestors. Devotees perform Tarpan and Shraddha rituals near water bodies and offer prayers for the peace of departed souls. The day also holds agricultural significance as farmers pray for abundant rainfall and good harvests. Married women visit their parental homes and prepare special dishes to share with the family.

events_2027.name: Raksha Bandhan
events_2027.description: Raksha Bandhan celebrates the sacred bond between brothers and sisters, with sisters tying a protective thread (Rakhi) on their brothers' wrists. In return, brothers promise to protect their sisters and offer gifts as tokens of love and commitment. The festival falls on the Purnima of the Shravana month and is accompanied by sweets, family gatherings, and festive meals. It transcends blood relations, as people tie Rakhis to friends and neighbors to symbolize bonds of mutual protection.

events_2027.name: Shravana Purnima
events_2027.description: Shravana Purnima is the full moon of the holy Shravana month, one of the most sacred months in the Hindu calendar. In coastal regions, it is celebrated as Narali Purnima, when fishermen offer coconuts to the sea god Varuna seeking a safe fishing season. Brahmins change their sacred thread (Janeu) on this day during the Upakarma ceremony. The day also marks the occasion for the Rig Veda Upakarma and Yajur Veda Upakarma in different traditions.

events_2027.name: Nikini Poya
events_2027.description: Nikini Poya marks the full moon of August and commemorates the first Buddhist council held at Rajagriha shortly after the Buddha's Parinirvana. The council, led by Mahakashyapa, compiled and organized the Buddha's teachings into the Tipitaka, preserving the Dharma for future generations. Sri Lankan Buddhists observe Sil (Eight Precepts), visit temples, and participate in meditation programs. The day serves as a reminder of the importance of preserving authentic Buddhist teachings.

events_2027.name: Raksha Bandhan Jain Observance
events_2027.description: Jain communities observe Raksha Bandhan with a unique spiritual dimension, as Jain priests tie sacred threads on devotees' wrists symbolizing protection from karmic bonds. The ritual emphasizes the protection of the soul from attachment and the bonds of worldly desire. Devotees visit Jain temples, receive blessings from monks and nuns, and renew their commitment to the five Mahavratas (Great Vows). The day combines the cultural celebration of sibling bonds with deeper Jain spiritual significance.

events_2027.name: Shravana Putrada Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Shravana Putrada Ekadashi, observed during the Shukla Paksha of Shravana, is especially revered by those desiring virtuous progeny. According to the Bhavishya Purana, this Ekadashi was observed by King Mahijit of Mahishmati, who was blessed with a son through its merit. Devotees worship Lord Vishnu with great devotion, offering fruits, flowers, and Tulsi leaves while maintaining a strict fast. It is also known as Pavitropana Ekadashi in some traditions.

events_2027.name: Krishna Janmashtami
events_2027.description: Krishna Janmashtami celebrates the birth of Lord Krishna, the eighth avatar of Vishnu, on the Ashtami Tithi of Krishna Paksha in the month of Bhadrapada. Devotees fast throughout the day and break their fast at midnight, the believed hour of Krishna's birth, with elaborate ceremonies and Aarti. Temples are decorated with flowers, and the famous Dahi Handi tradition in Maharashtra recreates Krishna's playful stealing of butter. Recitations from the Bhagavata Purana and devotional songs fill the night with spiritual celebration.

events_2027.name: Dahi Handi (Gopalkala)
events_2027.description: Dahi Handi is celebrated the day after Krishna Janmashtami, recreating young Krishna's playful stealing of butter and curd from the homes of Gopis. Teams of young men form human pyramids to break a clay pot (Handi) filled with curd, buttermilk, and butter hung high above the ground. The tradition is especially grand in Mumbai and Pune, where competitions attract thousands of participants and spectators. It celebrates teamwork, youthful energy, and the divine playfulness of Lord Krishna.

events_2027.name: Bhadrapada Purnima
events_2027.description: Bhadrapada Purnima is the full moon of the Bhadrapada month, observed as a day of heightened spiritual significance. In many traditions, this day is associated with the worship of Lord Satyanarayan and the performance of Satyanarayan Katha. Devotees fast, offer prayers, and visit temples to seek blessings for truth and righteousness in their lives. The Purnima also marks important observances in the Buddhist tradition related to the monsoon retreat.

events_2027.name: Daslakshan Preparation
events_2027.description: As the monsoon season progresses, Jain communities begin preparations for the most important festival season including Paryushana and Das Lakshana. Devotees increase their fasting days, attend more frequent temple services, and engage in deeper scriptural study. Monks and nuns deliver intensive discourse series on the Tattvartha Sutra and other foundational texts. The period leading up to Paryushana is marked by growing spiritual intensity and communal devotion.

events_2027.name: Janmashtami Extended
events_2027.description: In some regional traditions, Janmashtami celebrations extend to the Smarta observance date. Temples continue their celebration of Lord Krishna's birth with recitations from the Bhagavata Purana and enactments of scenes from Krishna's childhood. Devotees maintain their fasts and participate in Ras Leela performances depicting the divine play of the young Krishna with the Gopis. The extended celebration allows communities following different Panchang calculations to honor this most beloved incarnation of Vishnu.

events_2027.name: Aja Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Aja Ekadashi, observed during the Krishna Paksha of Bhadrapada, is named for its power to destroy sins as completely as fire consumes a forest. According to the Brahmanda Purana, King Harishchandra observed this Ekadashi and was restored to his kingdom after enduring great suffering. Devotees worship Lord Hrishikesha (Vishnu) with deep devotion and maintain a strict fast through the day and night. This Ekadashi is particularly recommended for those enduring hardships or seeking relief from suffering.

events_2027.name: Paryushana Begins (Shvetambara)
events_2027.description: Paryushana is the most important Jain festival, an eight-day period of intense austerity, fasting, and spiritual reflection observed by Shvetambara Jains. The word Paryushana means 'abiding' or 'coming together,' as monks settle in one place during the monsoon. Devotees undertake various fasts, attend daily discourses on the Kalpa Sutra, and practice the ten cardinal virtues. The festival culminates in Samvatsari, the Day of Universal Forgiveness, when all seek and grant forgiveness.

events_2027.name: Bhadrapada Amavasya
events_2027.description: Bhadrapada Amavasya is the new moon of the Bhadrapada month, a powerful day for ancestral worship and Pitru Tarpan. This is a rare Black Moon Amavasya in 2027, being the second new moon of the calendar month of August. Devotees perform Shraddha rituals, offer prayers at sacred rivers, and donate food and clothing to the needy. The spiritual intensity of this double-Amavasya month makes it especially potent for karmic purification.

events_2027.name: Ganesh Chaturthi
events_2027.description: Ganesh Chaturthi celebrates the birth of Lord Ganesha, the beloved elephant-headed deity of wisdom, prosperity, and new beginnings. Elaborate clay idols of Ganesha are installed in homes and public pandals, worshipped for ten days with modak sweets, flowers, and the chanting of Ganesh Atharvashirsha. The festival reaches its crescendo on Anant Chaturdashi when the idols are immersed in water bodies amid grand processions. Mumbai's celebrations are legendary, with towering idols and millions participating in the visarjan processions.

events_2027.name: Guru Ram Das Ji Parkash Purab
events_2027.description: This day marks the birth anniversary of Guru Ram Das Ji, the fourth Sikh Guru and founder of the holy city of Amritsar. He designed and began construction of the sacred pool (Amritsar) that would later house the Golden Temple. The Golden Temple is illuminated, and hymns composed by Guru Ram Das Ji, emphasizing humility and devotion, resonate in Gurdwaras. His Lavan composition, read during Sikh marriage ceremonies, remains central to Sikh wedding traditions.

events_2027.name: Samvatsari (Forgiveness Day)
events_2027.description: Samvatsari is the concluding and most significant day of Paryushana, known as the Day of Universal Forgiveness in Jain tradition. Jains seek forgiveness from all beings they may have harmed knowingly or unknowingly during the past year with the phrase 'Micchami Dukkadam.' Devotees perform elaborate temple rituals, attend the final discourse, and exchange messages of forgiveness with family, friends, and community members. It embodies the highest Jain ideal that forgiveness is the ultimate virtue for spiritual progress.

events_2027.name: Das Lakshana Begins (Digambara)
events_2027.description: Das Lakshana, the ten-day festival of Digambara Jains, begins on the day Paryushana concludes and celebrates the ten supreme virtues of the soul. Each of the ten days is devoted to one dharma: forgiveness, humility, straightforwardness, truthfulness, purity, self-restraint, austerity, renunciation, non-attachment, and celibacy. Devotees attend temple discourses, practice fasting, and reflect deeply on incorporating these virtues into daily life. The festival concludes with Anant Chaturdashi and the grand celebration of spiritual perfection.

events_2027.name: Parsva Ekadashi (Parivartini Ekadashi)
events_2027.description: Parsva Ekadashi, also known as Parivartini Ekadashi, is observed during the Shukla Paksha of Bhadrapada when Lord Vishnu turns on his side during his cosmic sleep. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, King Bali attained the Lord's grace by observing this Ekadashi with unwavering devotion. Devotees fast and worship Lord Vamana, placing the deity on a bed and symbolically turning the idol. This Ekadashi falls during the auspicious Chaturmas period and carries heightened spiritual significance.

events_2027.name: Das Lakshana Ends (Anant Chaturdashi)
events_2027.description: Anant Chaturdashi marks the final day of the Das Lakshana festival and the Ganesh Chaturthi celebrations. For Jains, this day celebrates the infinite qualities of the liberated soul and is marked by grand temple ceremonies and community gatherings. Devotees break their fasts and celebrate with feasts and cultural programs reflecting on the ten dharmas practiced during the festival. The conclusion is marked by a spirit of renewed spiritual commitment and joyful communal celebration.

events_2027.name: Onam
events_2027.description: Onam is the grand harvest festival of Kerala, celebrating the mythical return of King Mahabali, whose just rule is remembered as a golden age. The ten-day Thiruvonam festival features elaborate floral carpet designs (Pookalam), the grand Onasadya feast served on banana leaves, and exciting Vallam Kali boat races. Women perform the Thiruvathirakali dance in traditional white and gold attire around the Pookalam. The festival transcends religious boundaries, uniting all Keralites in a celebration of prosperity, equality, and cultural heritage.

events_2027.name: Bhadrapada Purnima (Proshtapadi Purnima)
events_2027.description: Bhadrapada Purnima is the full moon of the Bhadrapada month, observed by devotees with fasting, charity, and temple visits. In some traditions, it coincides with the beginning of Pitru Paksha, the fortnight dedicated to honoring deceased ancestors. The Purnima is also significant for the worship of Lord Satyanarayan and for performing Vedic rituals. Devotees believe that spiritual practices undertaken on this day yield manifold rewards.

events_2027.name: Binara Poya
events_2027.description: Binara Poya, observed on the September full moon in Sri Lanka, commemorates the establishment of the Bhikkhuni Sangha, the order of Buddhist nuns. It celebrates the Buddha's decision to ordain Mahapajapati Gotami and other women into the monastic order, recognizing their equal capacity for enlightenment. Buddhist temples hold special services honoring the contributions of women to the preservation and spread of the Dharma. It is a day for reflecting on gender equality within the Buddhist tradition.

events_2027.name: Pitru Paksha Begins
events_2027.description: Pitru Paksha, the fortnight of ancestors, begins after Bhadrapada Purnima and is dedicated to performing Shraddha rituals for departed family members. During these sixteen days, devotees offer food, water, and prayers to their ancestors through prescribed Vedic ceremonies. It is believed that the souls of three preceding generations visit their descendants during this period. Gaya in Bihar is considered the most sacred place for performing Pitru Paksha rituals.

events_2027.name: Ashwin Navapad Oli Begins
events_2027.description: The Ashwin Navapad Oli is the second of the two annual nine-day periods of heightened Jain austerity and devotion. Devotees worship the Navapad through special Ayambil fasts, eating only one bland meal per day without oil, spices, or milk products. The nine supreme entities worshipped during this period represent the highest ideals of Jain spiritual attainment. Communities gather for collective prayers and Swadhyay sessions, reinforcing the bonds of faith and shared practice.

events_2027.name: Indira Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Indira Ekadashi falls during the Krishna Paksha of Ashwin, within the Pitru Paksha period, and is specifically dedicated to the liberation of ancestors. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, King Indrasena observed this Ekadashi and liberated his father from the lower realms. Devotees combine their Pitru Paksha observances with this Ekadashi fast to maximize spiritual benefits for their ancestors. It is the only Ekadashi observed specifically for the welfare of departed souls.

events_2027.name: Sarva Pitru Amavasya (Mahalaya Amavasya)
events_2027.description: Sarva Pitru Amavasya is the most important day of Pitru Paksha, when Shraddha can be performed for all departed ancestors regardless of their death date. It is especially significant for those who could not perform Shraddha on the specific tithi of their ancestor's passing. Thousands gather at Gaya, Varanasi, and other pilgrimage sites to offer Tarpan and Pind Daan. The day marks the conclusion of the Pitru Paksha fortnight and the transition to the festive Navratri season.

events_2027.name: Gandhi Jayanti
events_2027.description: While primarily a national holiday, Jain communities honor Mahatma Gandhi's deep commitment to Ahimsa (non-violence) and truth, principles drawn directly from Jain philosophy. Gandhi was profoundly influenced by the Jain monk Shrimad Rajchandra and incorporated Jain ethics into his philosophy of Satyagraha. Jain temples and organizations hold special prayers and programs promoting non-violence and peace on this day. It serves as a reminder of how Jain values have shaped India's modern history and the global peace movement.

events_2027.name: Guru Angad Dev Ji Parkash Purab
events_2027.description: This day celebrates the birth anniversary of Guru Angad Dev Ji, the second Sikh Guru who standardized the Gurmukhi script used to write the Punjabi language and the Guru Granth Sahib. He emphasized physical fitness and established wrestling arenas (Mal Akhara) alongside spiritual practice. Gurdwaras hold special Kirtan and Langar to honor his contributions to Sikh education and community building. His devotion to Guru Nanak and humble service remain an inspiring model for Sikh disciples.

events_2027.name: Guru Gobind Singh Ji Gurgaddi Divas
events_2027.description: This day commemorates the installation of Guru Gobind Singh Ji as the tenth and final human Guru of the Sikhs at the age of nine, following the martyrdom of his father Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji. It is a day of mixed emotions, honoring both the sacrifice of the ninth Guru and the ascension of the young warrior-poet. Gurdwaras hold special prayers and Akhand Path to mark this pivotal moment in Sikh history. The day reminds Sikhs of the unbroken spiritual lineage from Guru Nanak to Guru Gobind Singh.

events_2027.name: Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji Shaheedi Divas
events_2027.description: This solemn day marks the martyrdom of Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji, the ninth Sikh Guru, who sacrificed his life in Delhi to protect the religious freedom of Hindus facing forced conversion by Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb. Known as Hind di Chadar (Shield of India), his sacrifice is one of the most selfless acts in religious history. Gurdwaras hold special Kirtan and Ardas, and Sikhs reflect on the values of religious freedom and sacrifice. His martyrdom directly led to the creation of the Khalsa by his son, Guru Gobind Singh.

events_2027.name: Sharad Navratri Begins
events_2027.description: Sharad Navratri, the most celebrated of the four Navratri festivals, begins on Ashwin Shukla Pratipada and spans nine nights of worship dedicated to Goddess Durga. Each night honors a different form of the Divine Mother, from Shailaputri to Siddhidatri, with specific colors, mantras, and rituals. Devotees observe fasts, perform Garba and Dandiya Raas dances in Gujarat, and conduct elaborate pujas at home and in temples. The festival culminates in Dussehra, celebrating the triumph of good over evil.

events_2027.name: Papankusha Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Papankusha Ekadashi, observed during the Shukla Paksha of Ashwin, is named for its ability to destroy sins as an elephant goad (Ankusha) controls an elephant. This Ekadashi falls during the Navratri period, amplifying its spiritual potency. Devotees worship Lord Padmanabha (Vishnu) with flowers, incense, and Tulsi leaves while maintaining a rigorous fast. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, observing this Ekadashi prevents the devotee from being taken to Yamaloka after death.

events_2027.name: Sharad Purnima
events_2027.description: Sharad Purnima is considered the brightest full moon of the year, when the moon's healing rays are believed to be at their most potent. Devotees prepare a special dish of rice flakes soaked in milk (Kheer) and leave it under the moonlight, believing it absorbs the moon's medicinal properties. According to legend, this is the night Lord Krishna performed the divine Raas Leela dance with the Gopis of Vrindavan. Temples stay open through the night for moonlight darshan and devotional singing.

events_2027.name: Vassa Ends (Pavarana Day)
events_2027.description: Pavarana Day marks the end of the three-month Vassa Rains Retreat, when monks invite criticism from their fellow monastics in the spirit of communal harmony. It is a day of great significance as the Sangha renews its bonds of mutual respect and accountability. Lay Buddhists celebrate by making merit through Dana offerings and attending special Dharma talks at their local monasteries. The conclusion of Vassa leads into the Kathina robe-offering season.

events_2027.name: Durga Ashtami and Navami
events_2027.description: Durga Ashtami and Maha Navami, the eighth and ninth days of Navratri, are the climactic culmination of the nine-night worship of Goddess Durga. On Ashtami, Sandhi Puja is performed at the exact junction of the eighth and ninth tithis, accompanied by 108 lamps and offerings. Kanya Pujan, the ritual worship of nine young girls representing the nine forms of Durga, is performed with elaborate ceremonies and gifts. These final days of Navratri carry the most intense spiritual energy of the entire festival period.

events_2027.name: Dussehra (Vijayadashami)
events_2027.description: Dussehra celebrates the victory of Lord Rama over the ten-headed demon king Ravana, symbolizing the eternal triumph of good over evil. Massive effigies of Ravana, Meghnad, and Kumbhakaran are set ablaze in towns across India during dramatic Ram Leela performances. In Bengal, the day coincides with Durga Visarjan, when the beautifully crafted Durga idols are immersed in rivers and lakes. It is considered an auspicious day for beginning new ventures, as the cosmic forces favor righteous action.

events_2027.name: Jain Diwali (New Year Eve)
events_2027.description: Jain Diwali commemorates the Nirvana of Lord Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara, who attained liberation at Pavapuri in 527 BCE. For Jains, the lighting of lamps symbolizes the light of knowledge that Lord Mahavira brought to the world, replacing the darkness of ignorance. Temples are illuminated and special prayers are offered in remembrance of Mahavira's final teachings before his liberation. The following day marks the Jain New Year, Vira Nirvana Samvat.

events_2027.name: Jain New Year (Vira Nirvana Samvat)
events_2027.description: The Jain New Year begins the day after Diwali, marking the start of a new Vira Nirvana Samvat era calculated from Lord Mahavira's liberation. Devotees visit temples early in the morning, perform prayers, and seek blessings for the coming year. Communities gather for cultural programs, exchange greetings, and share festive meals together. It is a day of joy and renewal, reflecting on Mahavira's teachings and resolving to live with greater compassion and non-violence.

events_2027.name: Ashoka Vijayadashami
events_2027.description: Ashoka Vijayadashami commemorates the historic conversion of Emperor Ashoka to Buddhism after witnessing the devastating aftermath of the Kalinga War. Moved by the immense suffering caused by his military conquest, Ashoka embraced the Dharma and devoted the rest of his reign to spreading Buddhist values of non-violence and compassion. Buddhist communities honor this transformation by discussing the power of Dharma to change even the most powerful rulers. The day serves as a reminder that compassion can triumph over violence.

events_2027.name: Karva Chauth
events_2027.description: Karva Chauth is a traditional fast observed by married Hindu women for the longevity and well-being of their husbands, falling on the Chaturthi of Krishna Paksha in the Kartik month. Women fast from sunrise until moonrise without consuming food or water, breaking the fast only after sighting the moon through a sieve while gazing at their husband's face. The ritual involves decorating hands with Mehndi, wearing bridal attire, and listening to the Karva Chauth Katha. In modern times, many husbands also fast alongside their wives in solidarity.

events_2027.name: Kathina Ceremony Begins
events_2027.description: The Kathina Ceremony is one of the oldest Buddhist traditions, a robe-offering ceremony held within one month after the end of the Vassa Retreat. Lay Buddhists present specially woven cloth to monks, which is then communally sewn into robes in a single day. This act of generosity is considered the highest form of merit-making in Theravada Buddhism. Each monastery holds its own Kathina celebration with feasting, Dharma talks, and joyful community gathering.

events_2027.name: Rama Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Rama Ekadashi, observed during the Krishna Paksha of Kartik, is named for its power to grant divine grace and spiritual merit equivalent to Lord Rama's blessings. According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, this Ekadashi freed a celestial nymph from a curse and restored her to heaven. Devotees worship Lord Vishnu with devotion, maintaining a strict fast and performing night vigils. It falls during the sacred Kartik month, amplifying its spiritual significance.

events_2027.name: Ashwin Amavasya (Kartik Amavasya)
events_2027.description: The Amavasya preceding Diwali is observed as a day of preparation and purification. Devotees clean their homes, perform ancestral rites, and prepare for the great festival of lights. Oil lamps are first lit on this evening to begin driving away darkness and negative energies. The spiritual cleansing rituals performed on this Amavasya are believed to prepare the space for Goddess Lakshmi's arrival on Diwali night.

events_2027.name: Dhanteras
events_2027.description: Dhanteras, also known as Dhantrayodashi, marks the beginning of the five-day Diwali celebrations and is dedicated to the worship of Lord Dhanvantari, the deity of Ayurveda and health. Devotees purchase gold, silver, and new utensils as symbols of prosperity and good fortune. In the evening, Yama Deepam lamps are lit facing south to ward off untimely death. Merchants clean their shops, prepare new account books, and pray to Goddess Lakshmi for a prosperous business year.

events_2027.name: Diwali (Jain Celebration)
events_2027.description: For Jains, Diwali is the anniversary of Lord Mahavira's attainment of Moksha (final liberation) at Pavapuri in 527 BCE. The eighteen kings present at his liberation lit lamps to compensate for the loss of the light of knowledge, establishing the tradition of Diwali illumination. Jain temples are beautifully illuminated, and devotees perform special prayers dedicated to Mahavira's final teachings. The Jain Diwali emphasizes that true light comes from within, through the knowledge and liberation exemplified by the Tirthankara.

events_2027.name: Narak Chaturdashi (Chhoti Diwali)
events_2027.description: Narak Chaturdashi commemorates Lord Krishna's victory over the demon Narakasura, liberating 16,000 captive women and bringing light to the world. Devotees wake before dawn to apply fragrant oil and take a purifying bath, symbolizing the cleansing of body and soul. Firecrackers are burst, and homes are illuminated with oil lamps to celebrate the destruction of evil forces. In South India, this day is the main Diwali celebration with elaborate rituals and family gatherings.

events_2027.name: Gujarati New Year (Bestu Varas)
events_2027.description: Bestu Varas, the Gujarati New Year, is celebrated the day after Diwali and holds special importance for the Jain community, particularly in Gujarat and Rajasthan. Business accounts are settled, new ledgers are opened with prayers to Goddess Saraswati, and families exchange greetings and sweets. Jain temples conduct special New Year pujas seeking blessings for prosperity, health, and spiritual growth. The day symbolizes fresh beginnings and the opportunity to live with greater adherence to Jain principles.

events_2027.name: Labh Panchami
events_2027.description: Labh Panchami falls five days after the Jain Diwali and marks the first working day of the new Jain calendar year. Business owners open new account books, perform puja in their shops and offices, and seek blessings from Goddess Lakshmi and the Tirthankaras for a prosperous year. It is considered the most auspicious day for starting new business ventures in the Jain community. Communities exchange greetings and sweets, and Gyanshala (Jain schools) resume their classes after the festive break.

events_2027.name: Diwali (Deepavali)
events_2027.description: Diwali, the Festival of Lights, is the grandest celebration in the Hindu calendar, marking the return of Lord Rama to Ayodhya after fourteen years of exile and his victory over Ravana. Homes are illuminated with thousands of oil lamps, candles, and colorful lights to symbolize the victory of light over darkness and knowledge over ignorance. Goddess Lakshmi is worshipped with elaborate pujas for prosperity and abundance, and families exchange sweets and gifts. The night sky blazes with fireworks as communities come together in a spirit of joy and renewal.

events_2027.name: Band Chhor Divas
events_2027.description: Band Chhor Divas, celebrated by Sikhs on Diwali, commemorates the liberation of Guru Hargobind Ji from Gwalior Fort along with 52 Hindu kings. The Guru refused to leave unless all the imprisoned kings were freed, earning him the title 'Bandi Chhor' (Liberator of Prisoners). The Golden Temple and Gurdwaras worldwide are illuminated with thousands of lights and fireworks. It is a celebration of freedom, justice, and the Sikh commitment to standing against oppression for all people.

events_2027.name: Il Poya (Deepavali Poya)
events_2027.description: Il Poya, the November full moon day, commemorates the Buddha sending out his first sixty enlightened disciples to spread the Dharma in all directions. It also marks the ordination of Rahula, the Buddha's son, and the arrival of Sanghamitta Theri in Sri Lanka with a sapling of the sacred Bodhi Tree. Sri Lankan Buddhists celebrate by observing Sil, visiting temples, and reflecting on the spread of Buddhism across Asia. The day emphasizes the Buddhist missionary spirit and the Dharma's universal message of compassion.

events_2027.name: Govardhan Puja (Annakut)
events_2027.description: Govardhan Puja celebrates Lord Krishna's lifting of Govardhan Hill to protect the villagers of Vrindavan from Indra's devastating rains. Devotees prepare an elaborate mountain-shaped offering of food called Annakut, presenting hundreds of dishes to the deity. In Nathdwara and Mathura, temples create stunning food arrangements symbolizing the hill of abundance. The day teaches the importance of protecting nature and standing against arrogance and destructive pride.

events_2027.name: Bhai Dooj
events_2027.description: Bhai Dooj celebrates the loving bond between brothers and sisters, observed two days after Diwali. Sisters apply a ceremonial tilak on their brothers' foreheads, perform aarti, and pray for their long life and prosperity. The festival is rooted in the legend of Yamuna and Yama, where the sister's love protected her brother from death. Brothers reciprocate with gifts and promises of lifelong care and protection.

events_2027.name: Prabodhini Ekadashi (Dev Uthani Ekadashi)
events_2027.description: Prabodhini Ekadashi marks the awakening of Lord Vishnu from his four-month cosmic sleep, ending the Chaturmas period. From this auspicious day, marriages, housewarming ceremonies, and other celebrations that were paused during Chaturmas can resume. Devotees celebrate with grand pujas, Tulsi Vivah ceremonies, and night-long Kirtans in Vishnu temples. The festival symbolizes the return of divine activity and the beginning of the auspicious wedding season.

events_2027.name: Kartik Purnima
events_2027.description: Kartik Purnima, also known as Dev Diwali, is the full moon of the sacred Kartik month when the gods descend to bathe in the Ganges. The ghats of Varanasi are lit with millions of lamps in a breathtaking display called Dev Deepawali. Devotees take ritual baths in sacred rivers, as bathing on this Purnima is considered equivalent to performing ten Ashwamedha Yagnas. It is also celebrated as Guru Nanak Jayanti by Sikhs and Tripura Purnima for the worship of Lord Shiva.

events_2027.name: Kartik Purnima (Jain)
events_2027.description: Kartik Purnima holds great significance in the Jain tradition as a day associated with the pilgrimage to Shatrunjaya, Palitana, and other major Jain Tirthas. Thousands of Jain pilgrims undertake the sacred climb to hilltop temples, performing special pujas and chanting devotional hymns. The full moon of the Kartik month is considered especially auspicious for temple visits, fasting, and charitable giving. It marks the conclusion of the Chaturmas period when monks and nuns resume their wandering.

events_2027.name: Guru Granth Sahib Gurgaddi Divas
