events_2027.description: This day celebrates the conferment of eternal Guruship upon the Guru Granth Sahib by Guru Gobind Singh Ji in 1708, declaring the sacred scripture as the everlasting guide for Sikhs. Gurdwaras conduct Akhand Path (continuous reading) of the entire Guru Granth Sahib, and elaborate processions carry the holy scripture through the streets. Devotees gather for Kirtan, Langar, and special prayers honoring the living Guru of the Sikh faith. It signifies that the divine wisdom contained in the Guru Granth Sahib transcends any human successor.

events_2027.name: Guru Nanak Dev Ji Jayanti
events_2027.description: Guru Nanak Jayanti, the most celebrated Sikh festival, marks the birth of Guru Nanak Dev Ji, the founder of Sikhism and its first Guru. Celebrations begin with Prabhat Pheris (early morning processions), followed by Akhand Path, Kirtan, and massive Langar serving food to people of all faiths. The Golden Temple in Amritsar is magnificently illuminated, and fireworks light up the sky. Guru Nanak's message of 'Ik Onkar' (One God) and the equality of all humanity is preached and celebrated globally.

events_2027.name: Loy Krathong
events_2027.description: Loy Krathong is a Thai Buddhist festival celebrated on the full moon of the twelfth Thai lunar month, when devotees float decorated lotus-shaped vessels (Krathong) on rivers and waterways. The floating Krathong, carrying candles, incense, and flowers, symbolizes letting go of grudges, anger, and negative thoughts. Buddhists use the occasion to pay respect to the Buddha, honor the water spirits, and release their worries downstream. The lantern-lit waterways create a magical atmosphere of peace, gratitude, and spiritual renewal.

events_2027.name: Terapanth Sthapana Divas
events_2027.description: Terapanth Sthapana Divas commemorates the founding of the Terapanth sect of Jainism by Acharya Bhikshu in 1760. The Terapanth movement emphasizes strict adherence to the original principles of Jain monastic discipline and non-violence. Followers gather for special prayers, recitations from the writings of Acharya Bhikshu, and community celebrations. The day serves as a reminder of the importance of reform, purity of practice, and unwavering commitment to Jain values.

events_2027.name: Utpanna Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Utpanna Ekadashi, observed during the Krishna Paksha of Margashirsha, commemorates the origin of Ekadashi as a divine feminine power who emerged from Lord Vishnu to defeat the demon Mura. According to the Padma Purana, this is the very first Ekadashi, and observing it is considered the foundation for all other Ekadashi fasts. Devotees fast with deep devotion and worship Lord Vishnu, recounting the story of Ekadashi Devi. It marks the beginning of the Ekadashi cycle in the new Hindu calendar year.

events_2027.name: Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji Shaheedi Purab (Nanakshahi)
events_2027.description: On this fixed Nanakshahi calendar date, Sikhs commemorate the supreme sacrifice of Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji. Gurdwaras hold special commemorative services with Kirtan describing his courage and commitment to protecting religious freedom. The Guru's final journey from Agra to Delhi, where he was publicly executed, is narrated in detail during the services. His sacrifice is remembered as one of the greatest acts of religious martyrdom in human history.

events_2027.name: Margashirsha Amavasya
events_2027.description: Margashirsha Amavasya is the new moon of the Margashirsha month, considered sacred for performing Shraddha rituals and ancestral worship. Lord Krishna declared in the Bhagavad Gita that among months he is Margashirsha, making this month especially holy. Devotees perform Tarpan, visit temples, and engage in charitable acts to honor their departed ancestors. Fasting and meditation on this Amavasya are believed to bring spiritual clarity and divine blessings.

events_2027.name: Maun Agiyaras (Silence Ekadashi)
events_2027.description: Maun Agiyaras is a significant Jain observance where devotees practice complete silence (Mauna Vrat) for the entire day as a form of spiritual discipline. The practice of silence helps control speech, one of the primary sources of karmic bondage in Jain philosophy. Devotees also fast, meditate, and study the teachings of the Tirthankaras during this period. It falls in the Margashirsha month and is widely observed as a day for deepening self-awareness and inner peace.

events_2027.name: Datta Jayanti
events_2027.description: Datta Jayanti celebrates the birth of Lord Dattatreya, the divine incarnation combining the trinity of Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva. Born to the sage Atri and his wife Anasuya, Dattatreya is revered as the Adi Guru who encompasses all three aspects of the divine. Devotees visit Dattatreya temples, perform paduka puja, and chant the Dattatreya Stotram. The festival is especially significant in Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Gujarat where Datta worship has deep roots.

events_2027.name: Bodhi Day
events_2027.description: Bodhi Day celebrates Siddhartha Gautama's attainment of Bodhi (enlightenment) under the Bodhi Tree in Bodh Gaya, becoming the Buddha. Observed primarily by Mahayana Buddhists on December 8th, the day is marked by meditation sessions, Dharma talks, and the decoration of Bodhi trees with lights and colored ornaments. Some practitioners stay up through the night in meditation, mirroring the Buddha's own experience of awakening at dawn. It is a celebration of the human potential for spiritual awakening and the beginning of the Buddhist path.

events_2027.name: Mokshada Ekadashi
events_2027.description: Mokshada Ekadashi, observed during the Shukla Paksha of Margashirsha, is renowned for granting Moksha (liberation from the cycle of rebirth). According to the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, this is the Ekadashi on which the Bhagavad Gita was revealed by Lord Krishna to Arjuna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Devotees celebrate it as Gita Jayanti, reading all eighteen chapters of the Gita and worshipping Lord Vishnu. It is considered one of the most spiritually powerful Ekadashis of the entire year.

events_2027.name: Gita Jayanti
events_2027.description: Gita Jayanti commemorates the day Lord Krishna delivered the sacred Bhagavad Gita to the warrior Arjuna before the great battle of Kurukshetra. This timeless scripture, consisting of 700 verses in 18 chapters, addresses the fundamental questions of duty, righteousness, and the nature of the self. Temples and ashrams organize complete recitations of the Gita, philosophical discourses, and meditation sessions. The teachings of Karma Yoga, Bhakti Yoga, and Jnana Yoga shared in the Gita continue to guide spiritual seekers worldwide.

events_2027.name: Margashirsha Purnima
events_2027.description: Margashirsha Purnima is the full moon of the Margashirsha month, one of the holiest months according to the Bhagavad Gita. Devotees observe fasts, perform Satyanarayan Puja, and visit temples to seek divine blessings. In many traditions, this Purnima is associated with the worship of Lord Vishnu and bathing in sacred rivers. Charitable acts and spiritual study undertaken on this day are considered especially meritorious.

events_2027.name: Margashirsha Purnima (Jain)
events_2027.description: Jain communities observe the Margashirsha Purnima with temple visits and special prayers honoring the Tirthankaras. Devotees perform elaborate Puja ceremonies, recite sacred texts, and engage in charitable activities for the benefit of all living beings. The full moon is considered a particularly auspicious time for meditation and spiritual advancement in the Jain tradition. Many communities organize Swadhyay circles to study and discuss the profound teachings of Jain philosophy.

events_2027.name: Maun Agiyaras (Winter)
events_2027.description: The winter Maun Agiyaras is observed with a vow of complete silence and fasting as a form of spiritual discipline. Jain monks, nuns, and lay practitioners spend the day in deep meditation, refraining from all speech and worldly activities. The practice of Maun (silence) is central to Jain spirituality, as it helps control the karmic inflow associated with speech. Devotees use this time for introspection, studying sacred texts, and cultivating inner peace.

events_2027.name: Saphala Ekadashi
events_2027.description: The second Saphala Ekadashi of the year falls during the Krishna Paksha of Pausha, closing the annual cycle of Ekadashi observances. Devotees worship Lord Narayana with coconut, pomegranate, betel leaves, and incense, seeking success and fulfillment in all endeavors. A night vigil is observed with devotional singing and reading of the Saphala Ekadashi Vrat Katha from the Padma Purana. The Ekadashi cycle ending and beginning in Pausha symbolizes the eternal nature of devotion and spiritual discipline.

events_2027.name: Pausha Amavasya
events_2027.description: Pausha Amavasya is the final new moon of the year 2027, a Somvati Amavasya falling on Monday, making it especially sacred. Devotees perform Tarpan for ancestors and visit sacred rivers for ritual bathing and charity. This Amavasya is considered an ideal time for closing the year's spiritual accounts and setting intentions for the coming year. Fasting and meditation are observed to purify the soul and prepare for the Makara Sankranti celebrations that follow.
